entries.short_definition: A government-to-government treaty enabling the FAA and a counterpart civil aviation authority to reciprocally recognize each other's airworthiness approvals, type certificates, and production approvals.
entries.full_definition:
A Bilateral Aviation Safety Agreement (BASA), and its narrower predecessor the Bilateral Airworthiness Agreement (BAA), is an executive agreement concluded under the authority of 49 U.S.C. Section 44701 by which the United States and a foreign sovereign agree to accept each other's findings of compliance with civil airworthiness, environmental, and production standards. Without such an instrument, every imported aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance would have to be re-certificated from scratch by the importing authority. The BASA framework allows the FAA to issue a validation type certificate based largely on the design approval already granted by the exporting authority, with attention focused only on a documented set of differences known as the Significant Standard Differences and Technical Issues lists.

The agreement itself is short and largely diplomatic. The operational detail lives in subordinate documents: Implementation Procedures for Airworthiness (IPA) for bilateral state-to-state relationships, and Technical Implementation Procedures (TIP) for the FAA's relationship with EASA. The U.S.-EU Aviation Safety Agreement was signed in Brussels on 30 June 2008 and entered into force on 1 May 2011 following the 15 March 2011 exchange of diplomatic notes; it is implemented through the FAA-EASA TIP, currently at Revision 7. The FAA-Transport Canada BASA dates to 12 June 2000 and includes a 60-day termination notice clause in Article 5. Parallel BASAs with Brazil's ANAC and China's CAAC are each implemented through their own IPAs covering design approval, production, export airworthiness approval, and post-certification continued operational safety.

Scope is product-specific. Annex 1 or the equivalent IPA schedule enumerates which classes of products are eligible for reciprocal acceptance: transport-category aeroplanes under Part 25, engines under Part 33, propellers under Part 35, technical standard order articles, and supplemental type certificates. The agreements typically extend to maintenance organization approvals (Part 145), flight simulator qualification, and pilot licensing only where a dedicated annex has been negotiated. Continued airworthiness obligations flow both ways: the State of Design issues the airworthiness directive, and the importing authority adopts or mandates it under its own regulations.

For a manufacturer, the practical effect is that a Helion-512 or any other Part 25 transport certificated by the FAA can be validated by EASA, TCCA, ANAC, or CAAC without a parallel ab initio program, provided the applicant addresses the published technical differences. The BASA regime is therefore the legal backbone of the global civil aerospace market, and its absence, as briefly experienced during the post-737 MAX trust gap between the FAA and EASA from 2019 through 2021, is what forces independent shadow certification campaigns.

entries.short_definition: DO-178C, "Software Considerations in Airborne Systems and Equipment Certification," is the 2011 RTCA/EUROCAE standard certification authorities accept as the means of compliance for airborne software.
entries.full_definition:
DO-178C is the joint RTCA/EUROCAE standard, published as RTCA DO-178C on 13 December 2011 and as EUROCAE ED-12C in January 2012, that governs the development and certification of software in airborne systems and equipment. It supersedes DO-178B/ED-12B, in force since 1992, and is recognized by the FAA through Advisory Circular AC 20-115D (issued 21 July 2017) and by EASA through AMC 20-115D as an acceptable means of compliance for the software aspects of type certification, supplemental type certification, and TSO authorization.

The standard organizes software life cycle activities into planning, development (requirements, design, coding, integration), and integral processes (verification, configuration management, software quality assurance, and certification liaison). Compliance is demonstrated against the objectives tabulated in Annex A, Tables A-1 through A-10. The required rigor scales with the Software Level, also called Design Assurance Level, which is allocated by the system safety assessment based on the severity of the failure condition the software can contribute to: Level A (Catastrophic) requires 71 objectives with 30 satisfied with independence; Level B (Hazardous) requires 69 objectives, 18 with independence; Level C (Major) requires 62 objectives, 5 with independence; Level D (Minor) requires 26 objectives, 2 with independence; and Level E (No Safety Effect) carries no DO-178C obligations. Higher levels additionally require structural coverage analysis at the statement, decision, and Modified Condition/Decision Coverage levels for Level A.

DO-178C clarified several DO-178B ambiguities (notably around parameter data items, deactivated code, and tool qualification) and introduced four technology supplements published in parallel: DO-330/ED-215 for Software Tool Qualification, DO-331/ED-218 for Model-Based Development and Verification, DO-332/ED-217 for Object-Oriented Technology and Related Techniques, and DO-333/ED-216 for Formal Methods. Supporting documents include DO-248C/ED-94C, which collects clarifications and rationale.

In practice, DO-178C compliance shapes program cost and schedule from the outset: the Plan for Software Aspects of Certification (PSAC), the five development plans, and the verification, configuration management, and quality assurance plans are negotiated with the certifying authority before code is written. Stage of Involvement (SOI) audits and the final Software Accomplishment Summary close out the credit claimed against Annex A.

entries.short_definition: DO-254 is the RTCA design assurance standard for airborne electronic hardware, published April 2000 and endorsed by the FAA as an acceptable means of compliance for certification.
entries.full_definition:
DO-254, formally titled "Design Assurance Guidance for Airborne Electronic Hardware," was issued by RTCA on April 19, 2000, prepared jointly by RTCA Special Committee 180 and EUROCAE Working Group 46. It is published in parallel by EUROCAE as ED-80. The document establishes the life-cycle processes by which complex custom hardware items, including FPGAs, PLDs, and ASICs, are developed to a level of rigor commensurate with their contribution to aircraft safety. It is the hardware counterpart to DO-178C, which governs airborne software.

The standard organizes design assurance into five levels keyed to the failure-condition classifications of ARP4761 and AC 25.1309: Level A (catastrophic), B (hazardous), C (major), D (minor), and E (no safety effect). Each level prescribes objectives across requirements capture, conceptual and detailed design, implementation, production transition, verification, validation, configuration management, process assurance, and certification liaison. Hardware is further classified as simple or complex, with simple items qualifying when deterministic test and analysis can demonstrate correct behavior under all foreseeable operating conditions; complex items at Levels A and B trigger additional means such as elemental analysis, safety-specific analyses, and advanced verification.

The FAA formally accepted DO-254 as an acceptable means of compliance with airworthiness regulations through Advisory Circular 20-152, issued June 30, 2005, initially applicable to Part 25 transport aircraft and progressively extended to Parts 23, 27, 29, and TSO authorizations. AC 20-152A, "Development Assurance for Airborne Electronic Hardware," released October 7, 2022, superseded the 2005 circular and substantially expanded the agency's position, adding guidance on commercial off-the-shelf components, COTS intellectual property, circuit board assemblies, and tool assessment. EASA's parallel position is documented in AMC 20-152A.

In practice, DO-254 governs how avionics suppliers structure their hardware programs, plans, standards, and certification data packages. Programs produce a Plan for Hardware Aspects of Certification, hardware design and verification plans, validation and verification reports, a Hardware Configuration Index, and a Hardware Accomplishment Summary. The standard does not prescribe specific tools or methods, but Designated Engineering Representatives and FAA Aircraft Certification Offices increasingly expect requirements-based verification, robust traceability, and quantified coverage at DAL A and B, making DO-254 the de facto floor for any electronic hardware destined for type-certificated civil aircraft.

entries.short_definition: A five-tier civil-aviation rigor scale (A through E) tied to failure-condition severity, dictating how exhaustively airborne systems, hardware, and software must be developed and verified.
entries.full_definition:
Design Assurance Level (more precisely Development Assurance Level, or DAL) is the qualitative classification that connects a system's worst-case failure-condition severity to the rigor of its development and verification process. It is established by the safety assessment process in SAE ARP4761 (originally issued 1996; ARP4761A jointly with EUROCAE ED-135, 20 December 2023) and ARP4754A/ED-79A, then invoked by software guidance DO-178C and hardware guidance DO-254. FAA AC 25.1309-1B (issued 30 August 2024, superseding the 1988 Rev. A) and 14 CFR 25.1309 anchor the scheme for Part 25 transport aircraft; EASA CS 25.1309 mirrors it.

The five levels map one-to-one to the five failure-condition categories defined in AC 25.1309-1B section 9: DAL A covers Catastrophic conditions, those that would prevent continued safe flight and landing (a definition tightened by Section 115 of the Aircraft Certification, Safety, and Accountability Act of 2020) and that must be Extremely Improbable, no more frequent than 1 x 10^-9 per flight hour. DAL B covers Hazardous (Severe-Major) conditions, with large reductions in safety margins, serious or fatal injury to a small number of occupants, and a budget of Extremely Remote, 1 x 10^-7 per flight hour. DAL C covers Major conditions, with significant crew workload increase or discomfort and a Remote budget of 1 x 10^-5 per flight hour. DAL D covers Minor conditions, slight reductions in safety margins that may be Probable. DAL E covers No Safety Effect conditions and carries no quantitative probability target.

Assignment is top-down. A Functional Hazard Assessment (FHA) at the aircraft and system level fixes the severity class; Preliminary System Safety Assessment then allocates DALs to items, with permitted reductions when independent architectural means (redundancy, dissimilarity, monitoring) are credited per ARP4754A Section 5.2. The level drives concrete deliverables: under DO-178C Table A-7, DAL A demands Modified Condition/Decision Coverage (MC/DC) of object code, DAL B requires Decision Coverage, DAL C requires Statement Coverage, DAL D has no structural coverage objective, and DAL E is outside DO-178C's scope. DO-254 imposes a parallel ladder on complex electronic hardware. Rotorcraft use AC 29-2C / AC 27-1B with comparable budgets, and engines fall under AC 33.75-1A; the DAL framework, not the probability number alone, is what auditors at the FAA Aircraft Certification Office and EASA review during type certification.

entries.short_definition: EASA is the European Union agency responsible for civil aviation safety, established in 2002 to issue type certificates and harmonize airworthiness rules across EU Member States.
entries.full_definition:
The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is the central technical authority for civil aviation safety in the European Union. It was created by Regulation (EC) No 1592/2002 of the European Parliament and of the Council, adopted on 15 July 2002 and published in Official Journal L 240/1, "on common rules in the field of civil aviation and establishing a European Aviation Safety Agency." The agency formally opened for business on 28 September 2003 and is headquartered in Cologne, Germany. Its original mandate covered the airworthiness and environmental certification of aeronautical products, parts, and appliances designed, manufactured, maintained, or used by persons under the regulatory oversight of EU Member States.

Regulation 1592/2002 was repealed and replaced by Regulation (EC) No 216/2008, which was itself superseded by the current basic regulation, Regulation (EU) 2018/1139. Under the 2018 framework, EASA's competence extends beyond initial airworthiness to flight operations, flight crew licensing, aerodromes, air traffic management and air navigation services, and unmanned aircraft systems. Detailed implementing rules sit underneath the basic regulation, most notably Part 21 for product certification and the CS series of certification specifications (for example CS-25 for large aeroplanes and CS-E for engines).

EASA Type Certificates are issued to the design organisation responsible for a product after the agency has verified compliance with the applicable certification basis. Once issued, an EASA TC is valid throughout all EU Member States and is the prerequisite for any subsequent Production Organisation Approval, individual Certificate of Airworthiness, or Supplemental Type Certificate. The agency also issues Type Certificate Data Sheets that record the approved configuration, operating limitations, and noise and emissions data.

International reach is provided through bilateral aviation safety agreements that allow reciprocal acceptance of design approvals and findings of compliance. The EU-US Bilateral Aviation Safety Agreement with the FAA entered into force on 1 May 2011, followed by Canada on 26 July 2011, Brazil on 27 August 2013, Japan on 22 June 2020, China on 1 September 2020, and the United Kingdom on 1 January 2021 following Brexit. A Certification Management Team arrangement with the FAA, established on 16 September 2015, governs day-to-day technical cooperation on shared certification projects.

entries.short_definition: EASA Certification Specifications CS-25 are the European airworthiness code for turbine-powered large aeroplanes above 5,700 kg MTOW, the structural counterpart to FAA Part 25.
entries.full_definition:
CS-25 is the body of Certification Specifications issued by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency that establishes the airworthiness standards a turbine-powered large aeroplane must meet to receive an EASA type certificate. Applicability is set by mass: any turbine-powered aeroplane with a maximum take-off mass exceeding 5,700 kg falls inside CS-25 rather than the smaller-aircraft code CS-23. The code was first issued by EASA on 17 October 2003 as a direct adaptation of JAR-25 Amendment 16, inheriting roughly three decades of Joint Aviation Authorities rulemaking, and it now derives its legal authority from the Basic Regulation, Regulation (EU) 2018/1139, and from the implementing rules on initial airworthiness.

Structurally CS-25 mirrors 14 CFR Part 25 almost section for section. Subpart B covers flight, including stall, controllability and performance demonstrations. Subpart C covers structure, including the limit and ultimate load factors, gust and manoeuvre envelopes, and fatigue and damage tolerance under CS 25.571. Subpart D treats design and construction, Subpart E powerplant installation, Subpart F equipment, Subpart G operating limitations and Subpart H electrical wiring interconnection systems. Each specification is paired with Acceptable Means of Compliance and Guidance Material that EASA publishes alongside the rule text, and the consolidated package is also distributed through the Easy Access Rules platform in PDF, online and XML form.

The current state of the code is Amendment 28, issued through ED Decision 2023/021/R on 19 December 2023, with a corrigendum applied on 20 November 2025. Amendment 28 revises ditching survivability under CS 25.563, refreshes the flight-crew interface requirements at CS 25.1302, and updates electrical-fire protection at CS 25.869, in each case to reflect lessons learned from in-service occurrences and recent certification campaigns. EASA harmonises CS-25 closely with FAA Part 25 through the bilateral safety framework, so most large transport-category programmes, including the Airbus A320neo and A350 families, are certificated against effectively parallel rule sets on both sides of the Atlantic. Significant deviations are recorded as Special Conditions, Equivalent Safety Findings or Certification Review Items in the type certificate data sheet.

entries.short_definition: The FAA regulation in Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations that prescribes procedures for certificating civil aircraft, engines, propellers, and articles in the United States.
entries.full_definition:
Part 21 of Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations, titled Certification Procedures for Products and Articles, sets the procedural framework under which the Federal Aviation Administration approves the design, production, and airworthiness of civil aviation products. Codified across Subparts A through P (sections 21.1 through 21.700), it governs how an applicant moves from a paper design to a flying article that the FAA recognizes as safe to operate. The companion airworthiness standards live in Parts 23, 25, 27, 29, 33, and 35; Part 21 is the procedural spine that binds them together (eCFR, 14 CFR Part 21).

A Type Certificate, issued under Subpart B and specifically section 21.21, approves the type design of an aircraft, engine, or propeller after the FAA finds that the design meets the applicable airworthiness, noise, fuel venting, and emissions standards, and that no feature makes the product unsafe for its category. Changes to an approved design follow Subpart D, and a third party seeking to modify a certificated product applies for a Supplemental Type Certificate under Subpart E. A Production Certificate, issued under Subpart G (sections 21.131 through 21.150), authorizes a holder to manufacture conforming articles under an approved quality system. An Airworthiness Certificate, issued under Subpart H (sections 21.171 through 21.199), is the per-aircraft document that authorizes operation; standard, special, restricted, and experimental categories all flow from this subpart (Cornell Legal Information Institute, 14 CFR Part 21).

Section 21.16 is the workhorse provision for novel vehicles. When the FAA finds that the airworthiness regulations do not contain adequate or appropriate safety standards because of a novel or unusual design feature, the agency issues Special Conditions that prescribe additional standards the applicant must meet. Special Conditions have been used for fly-by-wire transports, lithium-ion batteries on the Boeing 787, and powered-lift configurations such as the Joby JAS4-1, and are formally published in the Federal Register. The applicant and FAA also agree on Means of Compliance: the documented tests, analyses, and inspections that demonstrate each requirement is satisfied, captured in a certification plan negotiated early in the program.

Enforcement and surveillance run on parallel tracks. A Letter of Investigation, issued by an FAA inspector or aviation safety inspector, formally notifies a certificate holder that the agency is examining a possible regulatory violation and invites a written response before any sanction is proposed under 14 CFR Part 13. Part 21 also reaches into continued airworthiness through section 21.3 reporting of failures, malfunctions, and defects, and into international acceptance through Subpart L export airworthiness approvals.

entries.short_definition: FAA airworthiness standards for normal category airplanes up to 19 seats, restructured in 2017 around performance-based rules and industry consensus standards as accepted means of compliance.
entries.full_definition:
Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations, Part 23, sets the airworthiness standards the FAA applies when issuing type certificates for normal category airplanes with a maximum seating capacity of 19 or fewer passengers and a maximum takeoff weight of 19,000 pounds or less. It is the small-airplane counterpart to Part 25, which governs transport category aircraft, and it applies to powered fixed-wing designs ranging from light singles to business jets and, increasingly, to electric and hybrid configurations being certified under the same framework.

Amendment 23-64, published in the Federal Register on December 30, 2016 and effective August 30, 2017, retired roughly four decades of prescriptive design rules and replaced them with high-level safety objectives. The reorganized part is divided into seven subparts: A General (sections 23.2000-2015), B Flight (23.2100-2165), C Structures (23.2200-2270), D Design and Construction (23.2300-2335), E Powerplant (23.2400-2440), F Equipment (23.2500-2550), and G Flightcrew Interface and Other Information (23.2600-2620). Section 23.2005 establishes four certification levels keyed to maximum passenger seating: Level 1 for 0 to 1 passengers, Level 2 for 2 to 6, Level 3 for 7 to 9, and Level 4 for 10 to 19. Each level is further divided into low-speed and high-speed performance categories, with the threshold set at V_NO or V_MO of 250 KCAS and M_MO of 0.6. This four-by-two matrix lets the FAA scale rigor to the risk a given design actually presents.

Because the rule text is intentionally performance-based, detailed technical criteria live in industry consensus standards that the FAA accepts as means of compliance. ASTM Committee F44 on General Aviation Aircraft develops the primary set, anchored by ASTM F3264, Standard Specification for Normal Category Aeroplanes Certification. The FAA's acceptance process is defined in AC 23.2010-1 and tracked on the Part 23 Accepted Means of Compliance page, with the current list covering dozens of MOCs across F44 standards. Companion guidance, including AC 23-21 on compliance checklists for major alterations and the Amendment 23-64 Implementation Procedures Guide, walks applicants through showing compliance under the new structure. The FAA notes that novel architectures such as eVTOL or unmanned aircraft typically fall outside the accepted MOCs and require applicant-proposed alternatives or special conditions issued under Part 21.

entries.short_definition: Federal airworthiness standards governing the type certification of transport category airplanes, the design rulebook for jets and large turboprops operated in U.S. civil aviation.
entries.full_definition:
14 CFR Part 25, "Airworthiness Standards: Transport Category Airplanes," is the section of Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations that prescribes the design and construction requirements an applicant must meet to obtain a type certificate, or a change to a type certificate, for a transport category airplane. Section 25.1 states the applicability directly: the part applies to airplanes certificated under the transport category, and each applicant proceeding under Part 21 must show compliance with the applicable requirements. The transport category itself, as defined in Part 21 and the certification procedures, captures multi-engine jets with 10 or more seats or a maximum takeoff weight greater than 12,500 pounds, and propeller-driven airplanes with more than 19 seats or a maximum takeoff weight greater than 19,000 pounds. Aircraft such as the Boeing 737 and 787 families, the Airbus A320 and A350 families, and large turboprops including the ATR 72 are all certificated to Part 25.

Part 25 was added to 14 CFR effective February 1, 1965, replacing Part 4b of the Civil Air Regulations and recodifying Special Civil Air Regulation SR 422B, which had governed turbine-powered transports. Its structure has remained stable since: Subpart A (General, sections 25.1 through 25.5), Subpart B (Flight, 25.21 through 25.255), Subpart C (Structure, 25.301 through 25.581), Subpart D (Design and Construction, 25.601 through 25.899), Subpart E (Powerplant, 25.901 through 25.1207), Subpart F (Equipment, 25.1301 through 25.1461), Subpart G (Operating Limitations and Information, 25.1501 through 25.1587), Subpart H (Electrical Wiring Interconnection Systems, 25.1701 through 25.1733), and Subpart I (Special Federal Aviation Regulations, 25.1801). A series of appendices, including Appendix D on continuous turbulence and Appendix K on extended operations, carry specialized criteria referenced by the substantive sections.

The framework is amendment-numbered rather than rewritten: each rulemaking adds an amendment to the relevant sections and the certification basis for a given program is frozen at the amendment level in force on the application date, subject to the changed-product rule in Part 21.101. Recent amendments include Amendment 25-148, published at 87 FR 75710 on December 9, 2022, with a correction at 88 FR 2813 on January 18, 2023, and the 2024 rulemaking on equipment, systems, and network information security protection finalized at 89 FR 67428 on August 21, 2024. Where novel features fall outside the existing rules, the FAA imposes special conditions under 14 CFR 21.16; where the rule is met by other means, it grants equivalent level of safety findings. Together with Part 21 procedures and the Part 26 continued operational safety rules, Part 25 forms the backbone of transport airplane certification in the United States, and its text is harmonized with EASA CS-25 across most subparts. The eCFR maintains the controlling current version.

entries.short_definition: FAA airworthiness standard governing type certification of normal category rotorcraft with maximum weight of 7,000 pounds or less and nine or fewer passenger seats.
entries.full_definition:
Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations, Part 27, prescribes the airworthiness standards under which the Federal Aviation Administration issues type certificates, and approves changes to those certificates, for normal category rotorcraft. The applicability rule at 14 CFR 27.1 limits the part to rotorcraft with maximum weights of 7,000 pounds or less and nine or fewer passenger seats. Multiengine rotorcraft within that envelope may additionally be certificated to Category A operational standards if the supplemental engine-isolation, performance, and systems requirements referenced in Appendix C are met. Heavier or larger rotorcraft fall under Part 29 (transport category).

Part 27 was adopted on November 24, 1964 (Docket No. 5074, 29 FR 15695) as part of the recodification that produced the modern Federal Aviation Regulations, and it has been amended dozens of times since. It is organized into seven subparts: A (General, 27.1 to 27.2), B (Flight, 27.21 to 27.251), C (Strength Requirements, 27.301 to 27.563), D (Design and Construction, 27.601 to 27.873), E (Powerplant, 27.901 to 27.1027), F (Equipment, 27.1301 to 27.1461), and G (Operating Limitations and Information, 27.1501 to 27.1589). Key sections include 27.337 (limit maneuvering load factor), 27.561 (general emergency landing conditions), 27.785 (seats, berths, safety belts, and harnesses), 27.952 (fuel system crash resistance, added by Amendment 27-30 in 1994), and 27.1309 (equipment, systems, and installations).

Applicants demonstrate compliance through a combination of analysis, ground tests, and flight tests under a type certification project administered through the Rotorcraft Standards Branch in Fort Worth. Special conditions under 14 CFR 21.16 are issued when a novel or unusual design feature, such as fly-by-wire flight controls, lithium batteries, or electric propulsion, falls outside the existing Part 27 text. The 2023 rotorcraft certification rulemaking (88 FR 8728, February 10, 2023) modernized fatigue evaluation, occupant protection, and bird-strike provisions and harmonized several sections with EASA CS-27. Part 27 is the U.S. baseline for civil light helicopters including the Bell 505, Robinson R66, Airbus H125 and H130, and Leonardo AW109, and it forms the regulatory anchor for emerging eVTOL programs that seek rotorcraft type certification with attendant special conditions.

entries.short_definition: The FAA airworthiness code for transport category rotorcraft, prescribing type-certification standards for large helicopters above 7,000 lb or with 10 or more passenger seats.
entries.full_definition:
14 CFR Part 29, "Airworthiness Standards: Transport Category Rotorcraft," is the body of Federal Aviation Regulations that governs the design and type certification of large helicopters in the United States. Part 29 was originally codified at 29 FR 16150 on December 3, 1964 under Docket No. 5084, and is administered by the FAA Rotorcraft Standards Branch. Under 29.1, the part applies to rotorcraft seeking a transport category type certificate; in practice this covers machines above the Part 27 (Normal Category) ceiling, with the Part 27 boundary set at a maximum weight of 7,000 lb and nine or fewer passenger seats. Part 29 is the rotorcraft counterpart to Part 25 for transport airplanes, and certificates issued under it are processed under the Part 21 procedural framework.

The regulation is divided into seven subparts: A (General, 29.1 to 29.2), B (Flight, 29.21 to 29.251), C (Strength Requirements, 29.301 to 29.573), D (Design and Construction, 29.601 to 29.873), E (Powerplant, 29.901 to 29.1203), F (Equipment, 29.1301 to 29.1461), and G (Operating Limitations and Information, 29.1501 to 29.1589). Section 29.1 further partitions the fleet into Category A and Category B. Rotorcraft above 20,000 lb with ten or more passenger seats must be certified Category A, which mandates engine isolation, one-engine-inoperative performance, and continued safe flight after critical failures. Rotorcraft at or below 20,000 lb with nine or fewer seats may be certified Category B, which permits lower performance margins but still requires controllability and safe landing after engine failure. The current 20,000 lb threshold was set by Amendment 29-21 at 48 FR 4391 on January 31, 1983.

Compliance with Part 29 is the gating airworthiness condition for type certificates issued to aircraft such as the Sikorsky S-92, Leonardo AW139 and AW189, Airbus H175 and H225, and Bell 525. Where a design feature is novel or unusual, the FAA invokes 21.16 to issue Special Conditions supplementing Part 29; the agency's February 10, 2023 final rule (88 FR 8704) folded a long backlog of recurring special conditions into the rule text, modernizing Parts 27 and 29 to reflect fly-by-wire flight controls, composite structures, and high-energy rotor designs. Part 29 also interfaces with Part 33 for engine certification and with the operational rules in Parts 91, 135, and 121 that determine where a given category of rotorcraft may be flown commercially.

entries.short_definition: FAA airworthiness standards governing the type certification of aircraft engines, covering design, construction, and block-test requirements for both reciprocating and turbine powerplants.
entries.full_definition:
Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations, Part 33 ("Airworthiness Standards: Aircraft Engines") is the FAA rule that an applicant must show compliance with to obtain a type certificate, or a change to a type certificate, for an aircraft engine under 14 CFR Part 21. Section 33.1 establishes the applicability and, since the 1990 amendment, also cross-references the emissions standards of Part 34. Part 33 is the engine-side companion to the airframe airworthiness rules in Parts 23, 25, 27, and 29, and is the principal vehicle through which FAA Engine and Propeller Standards evaluates new turbofans, turboshafts, turboprops, and piston engines installed on U.S.-certificated civil aircraft.

The regulation is divided into seven subparts. Subpart A (33.1 through 33.8) covers general provisions, including ratings, instructions for continued airworthiness under 33.4 and Appendix A, and engine configuration definitions. Subpart B (33.11 through 33.29) sets design and construction requirements common to all engines, including materials, fire protection, and the Electronic Engine Control (EEC) provisions of 33.28. Subparts C and D contain the design and 150-hour block-test requirements for reciprocating engines (33.31 through 33.57). Subparts E and F apply to turbine engines (33.61 through 33.99) and include 33.62 stress analysis, 33.63 vibration, 33.68 induction-system icing, 33.70 engine life-limited parts, 33.75 safety analysis, 33.76 bird ingestion, 33.77 foreign object ingestion, 33.78 rain and hail, 33.87 endurance test, 33.88 engine overtemperature, 33.94 blade containment and rotor unbalance, and 33.97 thrust reversers. Subpart G (33.201) adds special requirements for Early ETOPS eligibility.

Part 33 has been amended repeatedly to absorb service experience and new technology. Notable amendments include 33-20 (1984) introducing rotor integrity rules, 33-23 (1998) revising the safety analysis and bird ingestion requirements, 33-26 (2007) overhauling 33.70 life-limited parts, and 33-34 (2014) adding Appendix D for mixed phase and ice crystal icing. Compliance is typically demonstrated through analysis, component rig testing, and the full-engine endurance and operability runs prescribed in Subpart F, with FAA Designated Engineering Representatives and Organization Designation Authorization holders witnessing key tests before a type certificate data sheet is issued.

entries.short_definition: FAA Advisory Circular describing acceptable means of compliance with 14 CFR 25.1309 system safety requirements for transport-category airplanes; current Revision B issued August 30, 2024.
entries.full_definition:
FAA Advisory Circular 25.1309-1, "System Design and Analysis," is the FAA's principal guidance document for showing compliance with 14 CFR 25.1309, the airworthiness regulation governing equipment, systems, and installations on Part 25 transport-category airplanes. Revision B was issued on August 30, 2024, cancelling Revision A (dated June 21, 1988) and was released in coordination with the final rule "System Safety Assessments" published in the Federal Register on August 27, 2024 (89 FR 68735). Revision B implements Section 115 of the Aircraft Certification, Safety, and Accountability Act of 2020 and incorporates roughly three decades of harmonization work with EASA CS-25 and ARAC's Systems Design and Analysis Harmonization Working Group recommendations.

The core construct of the AC is the inverse relationship between failure severity and acceptable probability. Chapter 7 defines five failure-condition severity classes and assigns quantitative safety objectives, expressed as average probability per flight hour: Catastrophic must be Extremely Improbable (no more frequent than 1x10^-9), Hazardous must be Extremely Remote (no more frequent than 1x10^-7), Major must be Remote (no more frequent than 1x10^-5), Minor must be Probable, and No Safety Effect carries no quantitative requirement. Per the Aircraft Certification, Safety, and Accountability Act, any failure condition that prevents continued safe flight and landing is classified as Catastrophic. Revision B also introduces explicit treatment of the "Catastrophic Single Latent Failure Plus One" (CSL+1) condition, requiring applicants to eliminate single-latent-plus-active failure paths to catastrophe to the maximum extent practical.

Revision B substantially expands the Fail-Safe Design Concept (Chapter 5), articulating principles such as designed integrity, redundancy, isolation, failure warning, and flightcrew procedures, and tightens documentation expectations around flightcrew response time, alerting, and assumed pilot actions. It clarifies acceptable analytical methods in Chapters 8 through 11, including Functional Hazard Assessment (FHA), Preliminary System Safety Assessment (PSSA), System Safety Assessment (SSA), Common Cause Analysis, and the use of ARP4754B and ARP4761A as accepted industry standards for development assurance and safety assessment.

In practice, AC 25.1309-1B is the document every Part 25 type certificate, amended type certificate, and supplemental type certificate program references when scoping safety assessments, allocating Development Assurance Levels to software (per DO-178C) and complex hardware (per DO-254), and negotiating means-of-compliance plans with the FAA Aircraft Certification Service.

entries.short_definition: Design-specific airworthiness regulations the FAA issues under 14 CFR 21.16 when existing standards lack adequate or appropriate safety requirements for a novel or unusual feature.
entries.full_definition:
A special condition is a binding airworthiness regulation that applies to one particular type design. Under 14 CFR 21.16, the FAA prescribes special conditions when it finds that the airworthiness regulations of subchapter C do not contain adequate or appropriate safety standards for an aircraft, aircraft engine, or propeller because of a novel or unusual design feature. The special conditions are issued in accordance with the rulemaking procedures of 14 CFR part 11 and must contain the safety standards the FAA finds necessary to establish a level of safety equivalent to that established by the existing regulations. They are published in the Federal Register, attached to the type certificate, and become part of the certification basis for that design.

In practice, special conditions are how the FAA closes the gap when a project outruns the existing parts. Parts 23, 25, 27, 29, 33, and 35 were written around piston, turbine, and conventional airframe configurations, and contain little or nothing on high-voltage batteries, distributed electric propulsion, tilting rotors, or full hybrid-electric architectures. For powered-lift eVTOL programs the FAA has used 14 CFR 21.17(b) special class procedures to construct a certification basis out of pieces of parts 23, 25, 27, and 29 plus design-specific criteria, as it did for the Joby Aero Model JAS4-1 with airworthiness criteria effective April 8, 2024. For novel propulsion units it has gone directly to 21.16, issuing special conditions for the Safran Electric and Power Model ENGINeUS 100A1 electric engine in late 2024 because part 33 was written for reciprocating and turbine engines and does not cover electric motors, motor controllers, or high-voltage power electronics.

A typical special conditions package is narrow and prescriptive. It cites the applicable type certification application, identifies the novel feature (lithium-ion battery installation, hybrid powerplant, fly-by-wire flight controls, composite primary structure, high-energy rotors), and writes new numbered requirements that the applicant must show compliance with alongside the underlying part. The FAA publishes a notice of proposed special conditions, takes comment, and then issues the final special conditions; they apply only to the named model and any later derivative the FAA designates. Special conditions sit alongside, not in place of, the type certificate under 14 CFR 21.21, and they are distinct from equivalent level of safety findings, exemptions under part 11, and the broader special class path of 21.17(b).

entries.short_definition: An FAA Technical Standard Order is a minimum performance standard issued for specified articles used on civil aircraft, with authorization granted under 14 CFR Part 21 Subpart O.
entries.full_definition:
A Technical Standard Order (TSO) is a minimum performance standard issued by the Federal Aviation Administration for specified articles, defined in 14 CFR 21.601 as a material, part, process, or appliance used on civil aircraft. The TSO itself is the standard; what manufacturers receive after demonstrating compliance is a TSO Authorization (TSOA), which 14 CFR 21.601(b)(2) defines as "an FAA design and production approval issued to the manufacturer of an article that has been found to meet a specific TSO." The program is administered under 14 CFR Part 21 Subpart O and FAA Order 8150.1D, effective 17 March 2017.

Subpart O lays out the full lifecycle of an authorization. Section 21.603 governs the application and statement of conformance. Section 21.605 covers organizational requirements, and sections 21.607 and 21.608 require a quality system and a quality manual aligned with the production rules of Subpart G. Section 21.610 covers inspections and tests, 21.611 the issuance of the authorization, and 21.613 its duration. Ongoing obligations sit in 21.616 (responsibility of holder), 21.618 (approval for deviation), 21.619 (design changes), and 21.620 (changes in the quality system). Section 21.621 provides for letters of TSO design approval for imported articles. Each TSO references the applicable minimum performance specification, typically an RTCA DO document or SAE standard, which the article must meet on the date of application.

A TSOA is not installation approval. Per Subpart O and FAA guidance, a TSO-marked article may only be installed on a particular aircraft after a separate showing that it meets the airworthiness requirements of that aircraft's certification basis, normally through a Type Certificate, a Supplemental Type Certificate, or a field approval. In practice, TSOs cover articles ranging from seat belts (TSO-C22) and life rafts (TSO-C70) to GPS receivers (TSO-C145/C146), ADS-B Out equipment (TSO-C166b), and lithium battery systems (TSO-C179b). The structure lets the FAA delegate the heavy lifting of article-level qualification to industry standards bodies while reserving aircraft-level certification for itself, which is why TSOAs are a near-universal gating step for avionics and equipment suppliers selling into the civil fleet.

entries.short_definition: ICAO is the United Nations specialized agency that codifies international civil aviation Standards and Recommended Practices through 19 Annexes to the 1944 Chicago Convention.
entries.full_definition:
The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) is the United Nations specialized agency that develops and maintains the technical and legal framework for international civil aviation. It was created by the Convention on International Civil Aviation, signed in Chicago on 7 December 1944 by 52 of the 54 attending states. A Provisional ICAO (PICAO) operated out of Montreal from June 1945 until the Convention received sufficient ratifications and entered into force on 4 April 1947, at which point ICAO formally came into being. Its headquarters remain in Montreal, and membership now stands at 193 Contracting States.

ICAO's governance is fixed by the Convention itself. The Assembly, comprising all 193 States, meets at least once every three years and sets policy and budget. A 36-State Council, elected by the Assembly, is the permanent governing body and is the organ that formally adopts technical standards under Article 54. Three subsidiary bodies prepare those standards: the Air Navigation Commission (ANC) for 16 of the 19 Annexes, the Air Transport Committee (ATC) for Annex 9 (Facilitation) and Annex 17 (Security), and the Committee on Aviation Environmental Protection (CAEP) for Annex 16 (Environmental Protection).

The substantive output of ICAO is the body of Standards and Recommended Practices (SARPs) adopted under Article 37 of the Convention and published as the 19 Annexes. The Annexes span the full operational stack: Personnel Licensing (Annex 1), Rules of the Air (Annex 2), Operation of Aircraft (Annex 6), Aircraft Nationality and Registration Marks (Annex 7), Airworthiness (Annex 8), Aeronautical Telecommunications including CNS and SSR codes (Annex 10), Air Traffic Services (Annex 11), Accident and Incident Investigation (Annex 13), Aerodromes (Annex 14), Dangerous Goods (Annex 18), and Safety Management (Annex 19, adopted 2013). Taken together the Annexes contain more than 12,000 SARPs. Standards are binding on Contracting States unless a difference is filed under Article 38; Recommended Practices are advisory.

ICAO also issues Procedures for Air Navigation Services (PANS, the Doc 4444 / Doc 8168 series), regional Air Navigation Plans, and the Universal Safety Oversight Audit Programme (USOAP) that benchmarks State implementation. National regulators, including the FAA and EASA, draft domestic airworthiness and operational rules to be consistent with the relevant Annex; deviations must be notified to ICAO and published in State AIPs.

entries.short_definition: A risk-based, ten-step methodology issued by JARUS for authorising uncrewed aircraft operations in the Specific category, calibrating evidence and design assurance to assessed ground and air risk.
entries.full_definition:
The Specific Operations Risk Assessment (SORA) is the harmonised methodology published by the Joint Authorities for Rulemaking on Unmanned Systems (JARUS) for authorising uncrewed aircraft system (UAS) operations that fall above the low-risk Open category but below the manned-equivalent Certified category. Edition 2.5, agreed at the JARUS Plenary in Almaty on 13 May 2024 and released as JAR_doc_25 in June 2024, is the version that European Union Member States adopted under Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2019/947 and that competent authorities outside the EU increasingly mirror. The package comprises the Main Body plus Annexes A, B, E, F, H and I, each carrying its own edition 2.5 release.

SORA proceeds through ten sequential steps. The applicant first describes the Concept of Operations (Step 1), then derives an intrinsic Ground Risk Class (GRC) from the operational volume, UAS size and overflown population (Step 2), applies mitigations to obtain a final GRC (Step 3), determines an initial Air Risk Class (ARC) from airspace characteristics (Step 4), and reduces it through strategic mitigations to a residual ARC (Step 5). Tactical mitigation performance requirements follow (Step 6). The final GRC and residual ARC are combined in the SAIL matrix (Step 7) to assign a Specific Assurance and Integrity Level from I to VI, where SAIL I corresponds to minimal residual risk and SAIL VI to risk approaching the Certified category. Steps 8 through 10 derive the corresponding Operational Safety Objectives (OSOs), adjacent-area containment requirements, and the comprehensive safety portfolio submitted to the authority.

Edition 2.5 restructures the v2.0 framework around a quantitative Target Level of Safety calibrated to manned aviation, replaces the single intrinsic GRC table with explicit handling of controlled and uncontrolled ground areas, and refines the OSO catalogue and SAIL-to-evidence mapping. Design assurance scales with SAIL: a manufacturer declaration suffices at SAIL I-II, a published means of compliance at SAIL III, a Design Verification Report at SAIL IV, and a full type certificate at SAIL V-VI. Annex F provides the theoretical basis for the ground risk model and Annex I covers the collision risk model underpinning ARC.

For specific-category operators, SORA 2.5 is the operative document: it sets the vocabulary, the numerical thresholds and the evidentiary depth that determines whether a BVLOS, urban or beyond-population concept of operations is authorisable.

entries.short_definition: MOSA is the US DoD statutory acquisition strategy, codified at 10 USC 4401, that mandates modular design and consensus-based open interfaces for major defense programs to enable competition and refresh.
entries.full_definition:
The Modular Open Systems Approach (MOSA) is the integrated business and technical strategy that the Department of Defense is required to use, to the maximum extent practicable, on major defense acquisition programs (MDAPs) that received Milestone A or Milestone B approval after January 1, 2019. The requirement was first imposed by Section 805 of the Fiscal Year 2017 National Defense Authorization Act (Pub. L. 114-328) and is now codified at 10 U.S.C. Section 4401, with parallel direction for milestone decision authorities at 10 U.S.C. Section 4402 and contract clause requirements at Section 4403. Implementation across the Services was reinforced by the tri-service memorandum "Modular Open Systems Approaches for our Weapon Systems is a Warfighting Imperative," signed by the Secretaries of the Army, Navy, and Air Force on January 7, 2019, which directed inclusion of MOSA-supporting standards in all future weapon system requirements, programming, and development activities.

The statutory definition at 10 U.S.C. 4401(c)(1) frames MOSA around four elements: (A) modular design using modular system interfaces between major systems, major system components, and modular systems; (B) verification that those interfaces comply, where available and suitable, with widely supported and consensus-based standards; (C) a system architecture that allows severable major system components to be incrementally added, removed, or replaced across the life cycle; and (D) compliance with the technical data rights provisions now at 10 U.S.C. Sections 3771 through 3775. The DoD MOSA Reference Frame (OUSD(R&E), May 2020) and the MOSA Implementation Guidebook (OUSD(R&E), 27 February 2025) operationalize this with five enabling principles: establish an enabling environment, employ modular design, designate key interfaces, use open standards, and certify conformance.

DoD seeks five primary benefits from MOSA: enhanced competition through severable, openly competable modules; faster technology refresh without redesigning the whole system; incorporation of innovation by reconfiguring assets against changing threats; cost savings and cost avoidance through reuse across the life cycle; and improved interoperability by allowing independent change of software and hardware modules. In practice, programs satisfy MOSA by aligning to consortium-curated open standards such as the Sensor Open Systems Architecture (SOSA) Technical Standard, the Future Airborne Capability Environment (FACE) Technical Standard, the Open Mission Systems (OMS) standard, and the C5ISR/EW Modular Open Suite of Standards (CMOSS); these standards anchor RFP Sections L and M evaluation criteria templates issued under the Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement to enforce the Section 4401 requirement at source selection.

entries.short_definition: SAE Aerospace Recommended Practice ARP4754A, dated December 21, 2010, sets the development assurance process for civil aircraft and their systems used in certification.
entries.full_definition:
SAE ARP4754A, titled "Guidelines for Development of Civil Aircraft and Systems," was released on December 21, 2010 by SAE International jointly with EUROCAE as ED-79A, superseding the 1996 original ARP4754. It defines a top-down development assurance process intended to minimize errors in aircraft and system requirements, architecture, and integration, and is recognized by the FAA as an acceptable means of compliance with the development assurance expectations of 14 CFR 25.1309, 23.2510, 27.1309, and 29.1309. The document was developed in the context of 14 CFR Part 25 but is routinely applied across Parts 23, 27, 29, 33, and 35, with EASA accepting it as AMC under CS-25 Book 2.

The standard organizes the lifecycle into planning, aircraft and system development, integral processes, and certification coordination, covering requirements capture, validation, verification, configuration management, process assurance, and certification liaison (Sections 4 through 8). Its central contribution is the development assurance level (DAL) framework, mapped from the failure condition classifications of AC 25.1309-1B: Catastrophic to DAL A, Hazardous to DAL B, Major to DAL C, Minor to DAL D, and No Safety Effect to DAL E. ARP4754A formally splits this into the Functional Development Assurance Level (FDAL), assigned at the function and system level, and the Item Development Assurance Level (IDAL), assigned to the hardware and software item implementing the function; IDAL may be reduced below FDAL when architectural independence is demonstrated through Common Cause Analysis. Appendix A enumerates the 70-plus objectives required at each FDAL.

ARP4754A operates as the apex of a recognized certification stack: it invokes ARP4761 for safety assessment (FHA, PSSA, SSA, CCA), flows requirements into RTCA DO-178C for airborne software and DO-254 for complex electronic hardware, and references DO-297 for integrated modular avionics. The FAA formally recognized it through Advisory Circular AC 20-174, "Development of Civil Aircraft and Systems," issued September 30, 2011, which states ARP4754A "is one acceptable method" for establishing a development assurance process. ARP4754A remained the governing revision until SAE released ARP4754B in December 2023 as an interim update aligned with ARP4761A, though many active certification programs and bilateral acceptance arrangements still reference Revision A by name.

entries.short_definition: SAE Aerospace Recommended Practice that codifies the safety assessment process for civil airborne systems, defining FHA, PSSA, SSA, and CCA methods to demonstrate compliance with 14 CFR 25.1309.
entries.full_definition:
SAE ARP4761, "Guidelines and Methods for Conducting the Safety Assessment Process on Civil Airborne Systems and Equipment," was first issued by SAE International's S-18 committee on December 1, 1996. It became the de facto means of compliance for the airworthiness safety objectives in 14 CFR 25.1309 (and the harmonized EASA CS 25.1309) for transport category aircraft, and was paired with ARP4754 (development assurance) and the software/hardware standards DO-178B and DO-254. The 1996 document is organized around four core analytical activities: the Functional Hazard Assessment (FHA), which classifies failure conditions as Minor, Major, Hazardous, or Catastrophic with associated quantitative probability targets (10^-5, 10^-7, 10^-9 per flight hour); the Preliminary System Safety Assessment (PSSA), which allocates safety requirements to architecture and components; the System Safety Assessment (SSA), which verifies that the implemented design meets those requirements; and the Common Cause Analysis (CCA), comprising Zonal Safety Analysis, Particular Risks Analysis, and Common Mode Analysis.

After roughly two decades of community use, SAE S-18 and EUROCAE WG-63 began revision work in 2004 and jointly published ARP4761A and ED-135 on December 20, 2023. ARP4761A expands applicability beyond CFR/CS Part 25 to cover Parts 23, 27, and 29 aircraft, as well as engines and propellers under Parts 33 and 35 (and CS-E, CS-P). It formalizes Aircraft Functional Hazard Assessment (AFHA), Preliminary Aircraft Safety Assessment (PASA), and Aircraft Safety Assessment (ASA) as dedicated aircraft-level processes that sit above the system-level FHA, PSSA, and SSA.

The A revision also introduces Model-Based Safety Analysis (MBSA) as an alternative to traditional Fault Tree Analysis, adds Cascading Effects Analysis (CEA) for multi-system propagation, and integrates Master Minimum Equipment List (MMEL) and in-service safety considerations. Notably, the assignment of Function Development Assurance Levels (FDAL) and Item Development Assurance Levels (IDAL) was moved out of ARP4754 and into ARP4761A, tightening the link between hazard classification and development rigor. ARP4761A is the cited means of compliance in FAA AC 25.1309-1B drafts and EASA AMC 25.1309, and is the companion safety standard to ARP4754B, which addresses the development process running in parallel.

entries.short_definition: An FAA design approval issued under 14 CFR 21 Subpart B that certifies an aircraft, engine, or propeller type design meets the applicable airworthiness and environmental requirements.
entries.full_definition:
A type certificate is the FAA's approval of a specific aircraft, aircraft engine, or propeller type design under 14 CFR Part 21 Subpart B. The TC documents that the type design complies with the applicable airworthiness, noise, fuel venting, exhaust emission, and fuel efficiency requirements of 14 CFR, and it forms the legal basis for any subsequent production approval or airworthiness certificate issued for an individual article built to that design.

Under 14 CFR 21.15, an applicant submits a type certificate application on a form prescribed by the FAA, accompanied for aircraft by a three-view drawing and available preliminary basic data, and for engines by a description of design features, operating characteristics, and proposed operating limitations. The certification basis is fixed by 14 CFR 21.17: the applicant must show compliance with the requirements of Parts 23, 25, 27, 29, 31, 33, 35, and 36 that are effective on the date of application, plus any special conditions issued under 14 CFR 21.16 to address novel or unusual design features. An application is effective for 5 years for transport-category aircraft and 3 years for any other type certificate; if certification is not completed within that window, the applicant must refile or extend by selecting a later effective date for the certification basis under 14 CFR 21.17(d).

Before any FAA inspection or flight test, 14 CFR 21.33 requires the applicant to conduct conformity inspections demonstrating that materials, parts, manufacturing processes, construction, and assembly all conform to the type design, and to allow the FAA to make any inspection and ground or flight test necessary to determine compliance. Flight testing required under 14 CFR 21.35 includes the operational and functional flight tests the FAA finds necessary, conducted after the airframe, engine, and propeller combination has been shown to be reliable and functioning properly.

When the Administrator finds the type design meets the applicable requirements and that no feature or characteristic of the aircraft makes it unsafe for the category sought, the FAA issues the type certificate under 14 CFR 21.21. Per 14 CFR 21.41, the type certificate is deemed to include the type design, the operating limitations, the type certificate data sheet (TCDS), the applicable regulations with which the FAA records compliance, and any other conditions or limitations prescribed for the product. The TC is the predicate document for production certificates under Subpart G and standard airworthiness certificates under Subpart H.

entries.short_definition: The regulatory pathway by which electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft earn FAA type, production, and operating approval under the new powered-lift category established in 2024.
entries.full_definition:
eVTOL certification is the process by which electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft, which transition between wing-borne and thrust-borne flight, qualify for civil operation in the United States. Because these aircraft are neither airplanes nor rotorcraft, the FAA certificates them as a third category called powered-lift, the first new civil aircraft category since the helicopter in 1947. The framework rests on two pillars: a per-aircraft airworthiness basis assembled under 14 CFR 21.17(b), and a category-wide operating and pilot rule finalized in 2024.

On October 22, 2024 the FAA signed the final rule "Integration of Powered-Lift: Pilot Certification and Operations; Miscellaneous Amendments Related to Rotorcraft and Airplanes," published in the Federal Register on November 21, 2024 and effective December 23, 2024. The rule promulgates a ten-year Special Federal Aviation Regulation (SFAR) that defines pilot certification, training, and operating requirements for powered-lift, applying airplane or helicopter standards on a performance basis depending on flight mode. It also permits initial training in single-control aircraft when paired with full-flight simulator time, addressing a chicken-and-egg problem that had blocked type-rating pathways.

Airworthiness itself is handled aircraft by aircraft under 21.17(b), which lets the FAA draw applicable requirements from Parts 23, 25, 27, 29, 33, and 35 and add novel performance-based criteria where no existing standard fits. Joby Aviation's JAS4-1 is certificating under a 21.17(b) basis after the FAA moved off a Part 23 starting point, and as of early 2024 the program had completed Stage 3 of the five-stage type certification process, advancing into for-credit testing in Stage 4. Archer Aviation's Model M001 (Midnight) received final special class airworthiness criteria published May 24, 2024 and effective June 24, 2024, covering a 6,500 lb maximum gross weight, pilot-plus-four configuration. Beta Technologies has pursued a staged path, advancing its CX300 conventional electric variant ahead of the ALIA-250 powered-lift to retire battery and propulsion risk on a Part 23 basis first.

The G-1 issue paper, which memorializes the agreed certification basis between applicant and FAA, anchors each program; revisions to a G-1 typically signal a change in regulatory approach, as occurred when powered-lift programs migrated from small-airplane to special class. Operators must additionally hold a Part 135 air carrier certificate, a Part 145 repair station certificate, and Part 141 pilot training authority before revenue service.

entries.short_definition: U.S. NRC regulation establishing reactor site suitability criteria, including exclusion area, low population zone, population center distance, and geologic and seismic siting requirements for power and testing reactors.
entries.full_definition:
10 CFR Part 100, "Reactor Site Criteria," is the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission regulation that governs the suitability of sites proposed for stationary power and testing reactors. It is split into two subparts by an effective date of January 10, 1997. Subpart A (sections 100.1 through 100.11) applies to stationary power reactor site applications filed before that date and to all testing reactors. Subpart B (sections 100.20, 100.21, and 100.23) applies to power reactor site applications on or after January 10, 1997, and is the operative siting rule for new builds licensed under 10 CFR Part 50 or 10 CFR Part 52.

Section 100.10 directs the Commission to consider population density and use characteristics, physical site characteristics, and reactor design when evaluating a site. Section 100.11 translates those factors into three quantitative zones. The exclusion area must be sized so that an individual at its boundary for the two hours immediately following the postulated fission product release would not receive a whole-body dose exceeding 25 rem or a thyroid dose exceeding 300 rem from iodine. The low population zone (LPZ) is the area immediately surrounding the exclusion area where protective measures are credibly feasible; the same 25 rem whole-body and 300 rem thyroid limits apply at its outer boundary for the duration of cloud passage. The population center distance must be at least one and one-third times the distance from the reactor to the outer LPZ boundary, with the population center boundary fixed by actual density rather than by political lines.

For post-1997 plants, Section 100.21 sets non-seismic site criteria (exclusion area authority, LPZ definition, population density limits, atmospheric dispersion, and emergency planning feasibility), while Section 100.23 requires explicit treatment of geologic and seismic uncertainty, typically via probabilistic seismic hazard analysis, and is implemented together with Appendix S to 10 CFR Part 50.

Appendix A to Part 100, "Seismic and Geologic Siting Criteria for Nuclear Power Plants," remains the controlling seismic rule for plants whose construction permit was issued before January 10, 1997. It defines the Safe Shutdown Earthquake (SSE) as the maximum vibratory ground motion for which safety-related structures, systems, and components must remain functional; the Operating Basis Earthquake (OBE) as the motion reasonably expected during plant life, historically set at no less than one-half the SSE; and a "capable fault" by surface movement within roughly the last 35,000 years, recurring movement within 500,000 years, or instrumentally recorded macro-seismicity. Investigations under Appendix A extend to a 200-mile regional radius around the site.

entries.short_definition: Nuclear Regulatory Commission regulation governing export and import of nuclear equipment, material, and related technology, prescribing general and specific license procedures under the Atomic Energy Act.
entries.full_definition:
10 CFR Part 110, "Export and Import of Nuclear Equipment and Material," is the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) regulation that prescribes licensing, enforcement, and rulemaking procedures and criteria for the export and import of nuclear facilities, special nuclear material, source material, byproduct material, and certain Category 1 and 2 radioactive sources. The Part derives its authority from the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (as amended), the Energy Reorganization Act of 1974, and the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Act of 1978, and implements U.S. obligations under bilateral agreements for peaceful nuclear cooperation. Section 110.1 sets the purpose and scope; 110.2 contains definitions; and 110.5 governs interpretations binding on the agency.

The regulation distinguishes two license types. A general license, established by rulemaking in Subpart C (sections 110.21 through 110.27), is effective without filing an application and authorizes routine exports of small quantities of source, byproduct, and special nuclear material, minor reactor components, and items in consumer products to most destinations. Section 110.28 excludes embargoed destinations (currently Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Sudan, and Syria) from any general license. A specific license, governed by sections 110.31 and 110.32 and applied for on NRC Form 7, is required for major facility exports (reactors, enrichment plants, reprocessing plants, heavy water production), bulk special nuclear material, and any export to restricted or embargoed destinations. Subpart D (sections 110.40 through 110.45) sets the review process, including mandatory Executive Branch review under section 110.41 by the Departments of State, Energy, Defense, Commerce, and the Arms Control disarmament agencies.

The scope of NRC jurisdiction is defined by an extensive set of appendices. Appendix A enumerates nuclear reactor equipment; Appendices B through H cover enrichment technologies (gas centrifuge, gaseous diffusion, aerodynamic, chemical and ion exchange, laser, plasma, electromagnetic); Appendix I covers reprocessing components; Appendix J covers uranium and plutonium conversion plants; Appendix K covers heavy water and deuterium production; Appendices N and O cover lithium isotope separation and fuel fabrication equipment; and Appendix P (added by the final rule effective August 27, 2010) lists Category 1 and Category 2 radioactive material thresholds aligned with IAEA Code of Conduct categories. Technology transfers and assistance to foreign nuclear activities outside the scope of Part 110 fall to the Department of Energy under 10 CFR Part 810, while dual-use commodities are licensed by Commerce under the Export Administration Regulations. Subparts G, H, J, and K govern public notification, hearings, classified information procedures, and rulemaking, respectively.

entries.short_definition: 10 CFR Part 50 is the NRC's legacy two-step reactor licensing rule, issuing a construction permit and then a separate operating license for production and utilization facilities.
entries.full_definition:
Title 10, Code of Federal Regulations, Part 50, "Domestic Licensing of Production and Utilization Facilities," is the foundational Nuclear Regulatory Commission rule for civilian reactor licensing, promulgated under the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 and Title II of the Energy Reorganization Act of 1974. Part 50 establishes the legacy two-step process under which essentially every currently operating US commercial power reactor was licensed: first a construction permit (CP) authorizing the applicant to build but not operate, and then, after construction is substantially complete, a separate operating license (OL) authorizing fuel load and power operation. Class 103 commercial licenses, held by all electricity-generating reactor facilities today, are issued under this framework (nrc.gov, Backgrounder on Nuclear Power Plant Licensing Process).

The technical content of a Part 50 application is defined by 10 CFR 50.34. Section 50.34(a) requires a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) at the CP stage, describing the preliminary design, site characteristics, principal design criteria, and preliminary safety analysis. Section 50.34(b) then requires a Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) at the OL stage, reflecting the as-built final design, technical specifications, and accident analyses. The key practical distinction from the one-step combined license under 10 CFR Part 52 is that Part 50 lets a utility begin construction with preliminary, not finalized, design information.

Part 50 carries the substantive safety appendices that anchor the wider US reactor regulatory regime. Appendix A, "General Design Criteria for Nuclear Power Plants," issued as a final rule on February 20, 1971, sets the high-level light-water reactor design requirements, beginning with Criterion 1 on quality standards and records. Appendix B, "Quality Assurance Criteria for Nuclear Power Plants and Fuel Reprocessing Plants," prescribes the eighteen QA criteria for safety-related structures, systems, and components. Appendix R, "Fire Protection Program for Nuclear Power Facilities Operating Prior to January 1, 1979," imposes prescriptive fire protection requirements on pre-1979 plants, including post-fire safe-shutdown separation provisions; later plants are governed by their license conditions and NFPA 805 (nrc.gov, Fire Protection Regulations in 10 CFR Part 50). Other notable appendices include Appendix I (ALARA effluent design objectives) and Appendix N (standardized multi-site CPs).

In practice, the entire operating US fleet, including units such as Vogtle 1 and 2, Palo Verde, and Diablo Canyon, holds Part 50 CPs and OLs; only the newer Vogtle 3 and 4 reactors were licensed under Part 52. License renewals beyond the initial 40-year term are handled under 10 CFR Part 54, but the underlying license remains a Part 50 instrument.

entries.short_definition: The U.S. NRC regulation, codified in 1989, that authorises a one-step Combined Licence for nuclear power plants alongside Early Site Permits and Standard Design Certifications.
entries.full_definition:
10 CFR Part 52, "Licenses, Certifications, and Approvals for Nuclear Power Plants," is the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission rule that consolidates construction and operating authorisation into a single proceeding. The NRC promulgated the original Part 52 on 18 April 1989 to add predictability to reactor licensing, and substantially revised it in 2007 to align with modern reactor designs. It runs in parallel with the older two-step process under 10 CFR Part 50, and applicants today generally elect Part 52 for new commercial reactors (nrc.gov).

Part 52 offers three principal instruments. Subpart A governs Early Site Permits (ESP), which approve a site's suitability under §§ 52.17 through 52.39 without committing to a specific reactor design and remain valid for 10 to 20 years. Subpart B governs Standard Design Certifications (DC), issued by Commission rulemaking as an appendix to Part 52; certified designs to date include the ABWR (Appendix A), System 80+ (Appendix B), AP600 (Appendix C), AP1000 (Appendix D), ESBWR (Appendix E), APR1400 (Appendix F), and the NuScale small modular reactor (Appendix G, published in the Federal Register on 19 January 2023 and effective 21 February 2023). Subpart C governs the Combined Licence (COL) itself, a combined construction permit and operating licence under § 52.97 that authorises both build and operation subject to verification (nrc.gov).

The verification mechanism is the Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria, or ITAAC, required by § 52.97(b) and § 52.99. ITAAC define the objective tests a licensee must complete before fuel load; most are inherited from the referenced Design Certification, with the remainder added as site-specific commitments in the COL. Section 52.103(g) requires the Commission to find, before operation, that the ITAAC acceptance criteria have been met, a determination that triggers a window for hearing requests under § 52.103(a) and (b) (nrc.gov).

The first COLs ever issued under Part 52 went to Southern Nuclear for Vogtle Units 3 and 4 in February 2012, referencing the AP1000 certified design in Appendix D; Unit 3 entered commercial operation on 31 July 2023 and Unit 4 on 29 April 2024. The NuScale rulemaking in Appendix G, certifying a 50 MWe module, is the first Part 52 design certification for a small modular reactor and provides the design that future COL applicants may incorporate by reference (nrc.gov).

entries.short_definition: 10 CFR Part 53 is the NRC's risk-informed, technology-inclusive, performance-based licensing framework for commercial nuclear plants, offered as an optional alternative to Parts 50 and 52.
entries.full_definition:
10 CFR Part 53, formally titled "Risk-Informed, Technology-Inclusive Regulatory Framework for Commercial Nuclear Plants," is a new optional licensing pathway codified by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission for advanced and conventional reactor designs. The Commission approved the final rule on March 26, 2026, the Federal Register published it on March 30, 2026, and it became effective on April 29, 2026, roughly 20 months ahead of the December 31, 2027 deadline that Congress imposed in section 103(a)(4) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act of 2019 (NEIMA), as reinforced by the ADVANCE Act of 2024. Rather than prescribing light-water-reactor-specific requirements, Part 53 sets safety objectives and lets applicants demonstrate compliance using a probabilistic risk assessment (PRA), other systematic risk evaluations, or a combination, with consensus codes and standards graded to the safety significance of each structure, system, and component (nrc.gov).

The rule is organised into roughly a dozen lettered subparts. Subpart A sets general provisions, Subpart B contains the safety requirements (including the quantitative health objectives that anchor the framework), and Subpart C governs design and analysis. Subpart D, "Siting Requirements," obliges applicants to evaluate external hazards such as geologic and seismic conditions, population factors, and emergency and security logistics, and to show that the site does not undermine Subpart B's safety criteria. A distinctive feature is that Subpart D permits siting in higher-density areas, including locations with more than roughly 25,000 nearby residents, provided the applicant demonstrates that societal risk is acceptable relative to societal benefits. Subparts E through K cover construction and manufacturing, operations, decommissioning, licensing processes, license maintenance, reporting, and quality assurance, and a later block of subparts (commonly cited as N through U) houses an alternative evaluation framework for risk insights (ecfr.gov).

Key differences from Parts 50 and 52 are structural rather than only procedural. Parts 50 and 52 retain a largely deterministic, prescriptive safety architecture (two-step construction permit and operating licence under Part 50; combined licence, design certification, and early site permit under Part 52). Part 53 keeps those licence vehicles but reorients them around performance criteria, mandates plant-specific PRAs, narrows design-basis accident analysis to the selection and functional criteria for safety-related SSCs, omits the Part 50.150 aircraft impact assessment, allows factory fuel loading for transportable reactors, and explicitly accommodates non-light-water technologies without bespoke exemptions. The detailed siting metrics referenced in industry summaries, including any design-basis ground motion and seismic design categorisation provisions, are implemented through Subpart D together with the accompanying regulatory guides; the precise section numbers and terminology should be confirmed against the codified text in the eCFR (federalregister.gov).

entries.short_definition: ARCAP is the U.S. NRC project that produced technology-inclusive, risk-informed guidance covering the non-safety-analysis content of advanced reactor applications under 10 CFR Parts 50, 52, and 53.
entries.full_definition:
The Advanced Reactor Content of Application Project (ARCAP) is the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission staff effort to develop technology-inclusive, risk-informed, and performance-based guidance on the content of license applications for non-light-water reactors. ARCAP builds on the Licensing Modernization Project (NEI 18-04) and is the regulator-side counterpart to the industry-led Technology-Inclusive Content of Application Project (TICAP), which is documented in NEI 21-07. With respect to the safety analysis report, TICAP guidance addresses most of Chapters 1 through 8, while ARCAP guidance addresses Chapters 9 through 12 and the non-SAR portions of an application. The framework supports applications for combined licenses, construction permits, operating licenses, design certifications, standard design approvals, and manufacturing licenses under 10 CFR Part 50 and Part 52, and informs the 10 CFR Part 53 rulemaking.

ARCAP was implemented through three principal draft regulatory guides. DG-1383, published in the Federal Register on September 30, 2021, endorsed the 2019 Edition of ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section XI, Division 2, "Requirements for Reliability and Integrity Management (RIM) Programs," with conditions for non-LWR application. It was finalized as Regulatory Guide 1.246 on November 3, 2022. DG-1413 proposed new Regulatory Guide 1.254, "Technology-Inclusive Identification of Licensing Events for Commercial Nuclear Plants," providing a method to identify event sequences from a probabilistic risk assessment and to classify them as anticipated operational occurrences, design-basis events, or beyond-design-basis events. DG-1414 is the companion guide for selecting the postulated bounding event whose consequence analysis informs siting, dose, and design-basis decisions, taking the licensing event set from DG-1413 as input.

The TICAP side of the framework was finalized as Regulatory Guide 1.253, Revision 0, issued April 3, 2024 (originally DG-1404), which endorses NEI 21-07, Revision 1, "Technology Inclusive Guidance for Non-Light Water Reactors Safety Analysis Report Content for Applicants Utilizing NEI 18-04 Methodology." Regulatory Guide 1.254, Revision 0, was issued in October 2024. Together, the ARCAP and TICAP guides give a near-term non-LWR applicant a complete, NRC-endorsed roadmap from PRA-derived event selection through SAR chapter content, replacing reliance on the LWR-centric Regulatory Guide 1.206 for technology-specific portions of an application.
