entries.short_definition: Thermal vacuum (TVAC) is an environmental test that exposes spacecraft hardware to high vacuum and cyclic hot and cold extremes to verify thermal design, workmanship, and functional performance.
entries.full_definition:
Thermal vacuum (TVAC) testing places flight hardware inside a vacuum chamber pumped below roughly 1e-5 Torr while shrouds drive the article through hot and cold temperature extremes that bound the on-orbit environment. The test simultaneously verifies that the thermal design works as predicted in the absence of convection and that workmanship defects, which thermal cycling tends to expose, are caught on the ground rather than in flight. Per NASA's General Environmental Verification Standard (GSFC-STD-7000, the GEVS), TVAC is required at multiple levels of assembly, with chamber pressure held below 1.33e-3 Pa (about 1e-5 Torr) and temperature extremes set a margin beyond the worst-case predicted hot and cold flight temperatures.

TVAC programs distinguish two complementary activities. Thermal balance demonstrates the thermal control system and correlates the thermal math model: the article is held at stable hot and cold plateaus until temperatures converge, and the recorded heater powers and gradients are used to anchor the model. Thermal cycling, by contrast, drives repeated transitions between hot and cold extremes to surface latent workmanship defects and verify functional performance across the operating envelope. GEVS calls for protoflight margins of +/-10 deg C beyond worst-case predictions, with cycle counts and dwells that scale with assembly level (for example, on the order of eight cycles at unit level and four at observatory level, with abbreviated functional tests at each plateau and full functional tests at hot and cold soak). A common protoflight rule of thumb is eight to twelve thermal cycles, with the larger number applied to lower assembly levels per GEVS Section 2.4 / 2.6.

A bakeout phase typically precedes or is folded into TVAC to drive off adsorbed water and condensable volatiles before contamination-sensitive surfaces are exposed. Bakeout temperatures and durations are selected against ASTM E595, which screens spacecraft polymers by exposing samples at 125 deg C and pressure below 7e-3 Pa (5e-5 Torr) for 24 hours and measuring total mass loss (TML) and collected volatile condensable materials (CVCM). The widely used screening thresholds, codified in GEVS and the NASA Outgassing Database, are TML at most 1.00 percent and CVCM at most 0.10 percent; materials that fail are either replaced or pre-baked until they meet the limits. For smallsats and CubeSats, programs commonly tailor GEVS to four to eight cycles with dwell times of two to six hours and a +/-10 deg C protoflight margin, while keeping the ASTM E595 outgassing limits unchanged because contamination physics does not scale with spacecraft size.

entries.short_definition: The U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices, first issued in 2001 and revised November 2019, set the baseline debris rules for NASA, FCC, NOAA, and FAA licensees.
entries.full_definition:
The U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices (ODMSP) were originally issued in 2001 as an interagency consensus document, developed under NASA leadership, to translate technical debris control concepts into binding policy for federally procured or licensed missions. The National Space Council approved a substantially revised ODMSP in November 2019, the first update in nearly two decades, in response to the rise of large constellations, CubeSats, rideshare deployments, and on-orbit servicing. The revised document retains the four original objectives and adds a fifth covering operations not contemplated in 2001.

Objective 1 governs debris released during normal operations, requiring that intentionally released objects be minimized in number, size, and orbital lifetime. Objective 2 addresses minimizing debris from accidental explosions, principally through end-of-mission passivation of stored energy in propellant tanks, batteries, and pressurant systems. Objective 3 covers safe flight profiles and operational configurations, including limits on the probability of accidental collision with large tracked objects over the mission lifetime and design provisions against loss of post-mission disposal capability due to small debris impacts. Objective 4 specifies post-mission disposal options: atmospheric reentry within 25 years of mission completion for spacecraft and orbital stages passing through low Earth orbit, maneuver to a disposal orbit (for geosynchronous systems, a graveyard orbit at least roughly 200 km above the GEO protected region), or direct retrieval. Objective 5, added in 2019, covers tracking and identification, large constellations (with elevated post-mission disposal reliability expectations), small spacecraft including CubeSats, and rendezvous and proximity operations.

The ODMSP is not self-executing. It is implemented by each U.S. agency through its own rules and licensing process: NASA through NASA-STD-8719.14 and the NASA Procedural Requirement on limiting orbital debris generation; the FCC through Part 25 commercial space station rules, as amended by the 2020 Report and Order and the 2022 five-year deorbit Second Report and Order (FCC 22-74); NOAA through the Commercial Remote Sensing Regulatory Affairs licensing regime; and the FAA through Part 450 launch and reentry licensing, with an ongoing rulemaking to extend debris requirements to on-orbit phases. International operators and the IADC Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines treat the ODMSP as the principal U.S. reference point. The authoritative text is maintained by the NASA Orbital Debris Program Office at orbitaldebris.jsc.nasa.gov.

entries.short_definition: The 1967 multilateral treaty governing State activity in outer space, establishing non-appropriation, State responsibility for national activities, launching-State liability, and a duty to avoid harmful contamination.
entries.full_definition:
The Treaty on Principles Governing the Activities of States in the Exploration and Use of Outer Space, including the Moon and Other Celestial Bodies, commonly called the Outer Space Treaty (OST), is the foundational instrument of international space law. Adopted by UN General Assembly Resolution 2222 (XXI) on 19 December 1966, it was opened for signature in Washington, London, and Moscow on 27 January 1967 and entered into force on 10 October 1967. The three depositaries (United States, United Kingdom, Russian Federation as successor to the USSR) maintain a status list that, as of 2025, records 115+ States Parties and roughly 20 additional signatories, including every major spacefaring nation. UNOOSA, the United Nations Office for Outer Space Affairs in Vienna, serves as the secretariat for the treaty and the Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space (COPUOS) that negotiated it.

Article VI is the hinge that pulls private actors into public international law. It provides that States Parties bear "international responsibility for national activities in outer space," whether carried on by governmental agencies or non-governmental entities, and that activities of non-governmental entities require "authorization and continuing supervision" by the appropriate State Party. This clause is the legal foundation for domestic licensing regimes such as FAA Part 450 for launch and reentry, FCC Part 25 for satellite communications, and NOAA's commercial remote sensing licensing under the Commercial Space Launch Act.

Article VII establishes liability for damage. Each State Party that launches or procures the launching of an object, or from whose territory or facility an object is launched, is internationally liable for damage caused by that object or its component parts on Earth, in air space, or in outer space, to another State Party or its natural or juridical persons. This launching-State concept was later elaborated by the 1972 Convention on International Liability for Damage Caused by Space Objects (the Liability Convention), which distinguishes absolute liability for surface damage from fault-based liability for in-orbit damage.

Article IX imposes substantive environmental and procedural obligations. States must conduct activities with "due regard" to the corresponding interests of other States Parties, and must pursue studies and explorations of celestial bodies "so as to avoid their harmful contamination and also adverse changes in the environment of the Earth resulting from the introduction of extraterrestrial matter." Where a planned activity or experiment "would cause potentially harmful interference" with the activities of other States Parties, the acting State must undertake "appropriate international consultations" before proceeding; a potentially affected State may likewise request consultations. Article IX is the textual anchor for planetary protection policy and, increasingly, for arguments about orbital debris mitigation under the IADC and ODMSP frameworks.

entries.short_definition: USML Category XV is the ITAR control list at 22 CFR 121.1 covering spacecraft and related articles whose export is licensed by the State Department's DDTC.
entries.full_definition:
USML Category XV (Spacecraft and Related Articles) is the section of the United States Munitions List, codified at 22 CFR 121.1, that designates spacecraft and directly related technical data and defense services as ITAR-controlled defense articles. Items in this category are subject to licensing by the State Department's Directorate of Defense Trade Controls (DDTC, at pmddtc.state.gov), rather than by the Commerce Department's Bureau of Industry and Security. The category is organized into paragraphs that enumerate end-items, ground control equipment, parts and components, and supporting technical data, with paragraphs (a) through (e) listing articles and the technical data paragraph capturing information directly related to them. Export, reexport, and brokering of Cat XV items require ITAR authorization under 22 CFR Parts 123, 124, and 125.

Following Section 1261 of the FY2013 NDAA, which repealed the blanket statutory requirement (originating in the 1999 Strom Thurmond NDAA, Section 1513) that all satellites be ITAR-controlled, State and Commerce issued companion rules on May 13, 2014 to restructure Category XV. The State Department's interim final rule (79 FR 27180) became effective November 10, 2014, with paragraph (d) on radiation-hardened microelectronics effective June 27, 2014; a follow-on final rule (82 FR 2889) took effect January 15, 2017. After reform, Cat XV retains spacecraft and components specially designed for military, intelligence, or otherwise sensitive missions, including items such as MILSATCOM with anti-jam features, certain remote sensing satellites that exceed performance thresholds enumerated in paragraph (a), classified spacecraft, and specified radiation-hardened microelectronics in paragraph (d). Commercial communications, scientific, and most civil remote sensing satellites no longer warranting USML control were transferred to the Commerce Control List under the new 9x515 ECCNs (9A515, 9B515, 9C515, 9D515, 9E515) at 15 CFR 774.

Defense services tied to Cat XV items are controlled under the ITAR definition at 22 CFR 120.32 (formerly 120.9) and typically require a Technical Assistance Agreement under 22 CFR Part 124 before a U.S. person may furnish assistance to a foreign person in the design, development, integration, launch, or operation of a covered spacecraft. The 9x515 ECCNs sit alongside Cat XV in the order of review at Supplement No. 4 to 15 CFR Part 774: if an item is described in Cat XV it is ITAR-controlled, and only items outside Cat XV are evaluated against 9x515 and the rest of the CCL. The 2014 reforms preserved the principle that classified technical data directly related to Cat XV items, and defense services using that classified data, remain ITAR-controlled regardless of where the hardware itself is classified.
